FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study baseline data (2016, 21 sites, ages 9-11, n=15,022), Data Release 2.0 (NDA Study 634). ABCD data repository cited explicitly.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using mixed-effects regression models with DEAP software; reports regression coefficients, SEs, and p-values for family income predicting screen time.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Information (Basel), 2020; no indication of duplicate or preprint of an included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable has its own coefficient anywhere in the paper. Step 2: The screen time DV is a multi-device aggregate — Methods state parents reported time 'using different types of electronic devices (computer, cellphone, tablet, iPod, and other devices)' summed as a single weekday hours measure (screentime_ss_weekday). This bundles non-phone devices (computer, tablet, iPod) with cellphone without any per-device breakout. Fails Step 2 as multi-device aggregated time. Additionally, the only predictors analyzed are family income and race (demographics), so even if screen time qualified, the other-side would be demographic-only — not individual-trait-level.

notes: Exclusion code: FT-EC2. Screen time is a multi-device aggregate (computer + cellphone + tablet + iPod + other) with no phone-specific breakout; also, the only predictors are demographics (family income, race), so the other-side individual-trait requirement would also fail independently.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, n=11,670 participants aged 9-10, Data Release 2.0 (NDA Study 634), 21 sites across 17 states. Explicitly named as 'Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study.'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) to assess associations between sleep disturbance and mental health outcomes at baseline and 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2020; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time appears only as a covariate in the sleep-mental health models, not as an IV or DV with its own reported coefficient. Methods state screen time (parent-reported time on computer, cellphone, tablet, iPod, and other devices) was included as a control variable. Results explicitly state: 'screen time was not included in the final models' after it failed to significantly improve model fit. No analysis in main text, supplementary, or sensitivity analyses has screen time as IV or DV with its own coefficient. SM/screen time as covariate/mediator/moderator-only fails FT-IC2.

notes: Exclusion code: FT-EC2. The paper's substantive analysis is sleep disturbance predicting mental health (depression, internalizing, externalizing). Screen time was tested as a covariate but dropped from final models for lack of model improvement. No qualifying SM/phone variable with its own coefficient appears anywhere.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study Year 2 (2018-2020), ages 10-13, n=7,614, 21 U.S. sites, 6.0 data release. Explicitly identified as the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional generalized additive mixed models (GAMM) and vertexwise FEMA analysis relating social media use and social media addiction to cortical morphology (thickness, surface area, volume) in ABCD Year 2 data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in NeuroImage, 2026; no indication of duplicate or preprint of an included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Social media use (hours per day, named platforms Instagram/Facebook/Twitter) is the IV in all main analyses, paired with brain morphology (cortical thickness, surface area, volume — individual-trait neural outcomes) as DV. Numeric estimates present throughout, e.g., 100x standardized B = -1.916, SE = 0.931, p = 0.040 for right hemisphere cortical volume; multiple ROI thickness coefficients in Table 2; vertexwise FEMA betas. Social media addiction questionnaire (SMAQ adapted from Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale) also analyzed as IV. Other side is brain morphology — a neural individual-trait variable. Qualifies under Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: standardized beta coefficients, SEs, p-values, and FDR-corrected Q-values in Table 2 for ROI analyses; vertexwise FEMA Z-scored beta coefficients in Fig. 2; Spearman's rho = 0.67 (p < 2.2e-16) between SM use and SM addiction reported in Results.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis performed with generalized additive mixed models (ROI) and FEMA (vertexwise), both relating qualifying SM variable (social media use hours/day) to individual-trait neural outcome (cortical morphology). Numeric results reported throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (Year 2 data; authors explicitly state 'secondary analysis of cross-sectional data'). Social media is the IV (not DV) in all analyses. EC7 requires longitudinal design with SM as DV only — neither condition holds here.
notes: INCLUDE. Clear qualifying analysis: social media use (hours/day) and social media addiction (SMAQ) as IVs predicting brain morphology (cortical thickness, surface area, volume) as DV. Cross-sectional, SM as IV, FT-EC7 does not apply.
